refer to a psychometrically validated experience it's nominally divorced from any so-called
supernatural, spiritual in that sense, or religious beliefs. It goes back having roots with William James. He was fascinated by the idea that these different
accounts of extraordinary human experiences across the centuries, across different parts
of the globes, described in different languages, associated with different religions, and outside
of religion, had these remarkable commonalities. And these included an overwhelming sense of
unity, positive mood, transcending time and space, and ineffability or the sense that
the experience was beyond words. And I'm showing you here this MEQ30 is a scale
we developed based on Psilocybin data, which is the first psychometrically developed scale
that's actually designed to assess this type of mystical experience from an acute experience. So you could-- you also use that for meditation
or other experiences as well, so that's that's out there. But nonetheless we found time and time again
now that the rates of those types of experiences are extremely high in a dose-related way with
psilocybin. We've also found that across those two initial
studies that psilocybin increases personality dimension of openness, and the definition
of personality is that it doesn't change. We know there are certain things, like getting
older changes it, in fact that decreases on average personality openness and certain things
and certain clinical treatments can have an effect, but this is the first example that
we are aware of, of any relatively discreet experimental manipulation ever changing a
validated personality construct. And openness refers to a broad-minded tolerance
to other people's points of view, the ability to see things from a both/ and rather than
an either/or framework, appreciation for aesthetics. So we found that this was increased over--,
with psilocybin, and not just from getting psilocybin. Digging into the data even amongst people
who got a high dose of psilocybin, it was the people who had a full mystical experience
defined by meeting those criteria for those various dimensions that I mentioned. People who had the same dose of psilocybin,
but who didn't have that type of subjective experience, we saw no change. So it really seems to be driven by the nature
of the experience that one has, not just getting the drug, and you'll see that theme crop up
again and again. Moving on to some of our clinical work. This was our cancer patients, cancer study,
picking up on older research done with LSD for cancer patients in the 1960s. These were 51 cancer patients. They all had life-threatening cancer they
all had a DSM-IV diagnosis either of involving depressed mood, some of which was major depressive
disorder, or one of the anxiety disorders. So about a third had a depressive disorder,
third anxiety, about a third a combo of both. In fact--, yeah in fact I think one day you
know we're gonna have existential distress or something like that in the DSM. There's some reason to think it's scientifically
distinct as a construct from garden-variety depression/anxiety. There's something about existential distress
with a potentially terminal illness. So the study design was within subjects. Everyone had two psilocybin sessions. One was a high dose, the real deal, and one
was a trivial dose really meant to just serve to control for expectancy. People might feel a little bit of something
but maybe even not. So essentially a placebo. So people had screening, and then they were
randomized to get either the high dose first and then a follow-up five weeks later and
then the trivial dose, the comparison dose, or the opposite order of that, and then another
follow-up for clinical symptoms five weeks later and then a six-month follow-up. And here I'm showing you the outcomes on our
Hamilton Depression and Anxiety Inventory, some gold standard measures, and we looked
at others and they showed the same story. But people came in at clinically severe levels
you trust me on that if you're not familiar with these these levels. They--, both groups look the same before randomization
so that's good, very high levels those randomized to the high dose group post one is five weeks
after the the first session, and so you see dramatic reductions into the non-clinical,
most people are not satisfying clinical criteria at this point. This is a squarely normal range, dramatic
production. The folks in the trivial dose group, the low
dose group, also saw a significant reduction. Probably through the rapport building, and
what we call the placebo effect, but nonetheless nowhere near and statistically differentiated
from the high-dose group. And then amazingly 10 weeks after that. So now this is five weeks after everyone's
gotten that second dose so now everyone has gotten the real deal high dose, whether it
was five weeks or ten weeks ago, and now both randomized groups are down to that extremely
low level of clinical distress. And the same basic story plays out whether
it's depression on the left or anxiety on the right. Huge effect sizes, showing you at the bottom
for those statistically inclined, and continuing with that theme we see that the clinical--,
that the mystical experience assessed through subjective questionnaires at the end of the
session day, showed statistically significant correlations to the reduction in both depression
and anxiety long-term. So it's not just us, I can't get into the
details, but a very similar study done with a somewhat smaller a group of folks done by
our good colleagues at New York University found extremely similar, very large, of a
similar size large effect size with a slightly different design. We publish those at the same time. Some other work with an open label pilot conducted
by our colleagues at an Imperial College London, very similar. They found people-- these were not cancer
patients but major depressive disorder outside of cancer-- found large reductions on depression
that appeared to be sustained even out to three months. And showing you we've recently had it accepted
into JAMA psychiatry, but we've done with major depression, outside of cancer again,
a randomized trial in the form of a waitlist control randomization study. Found again very large reductions in depressive
symptoms in major depressive patients. So keep your eye out for those results soon. I'll move into our addiction work, showing
you just one of the rationales for this work. The meta-analysis of older LSD studies for
the treatment of alcoholism showing you the odds ratio of improvement, so showing you
here that across those six rand-- the only six randomized studies in the field that there
was a statistical effect that equated to essentially doubling the odds of success at the first
follow-up compared to whatever the different comparison conditions were, which differed
across those studies. And in aggregate extremely significant, and
you know, more importantly, the large effect size. Again a doubling of the odds ratio of about
two. So also some older work on LSD treating heroin
addiction, which looked promising. One study on that. So I looked into starting very early out saying
hey what about smoking? This appears to be this cross addiction signal
I had a lot of experience in smoking. Very quantifiable, I kind of see it as a clean
form of addiction. It's hardcore addiction, but typically no
one's like wife or husband has left them, you know, their career's intact, so it's like
the pure self-control problem with that. I and probably some of my delayed discounting
colleagues on the call would sort of probably agree is-- see really is a heart of addiction. This, you know, what's good for you now, what's
good for you later, being in competition very much at play with smoking. So let's try that so it's a-- this was an
open label study didn't have a lot of money to throw at it. So besides being a good model of addiction,
it's also kind of a clinical problem itself, if you think about mortality or deaths. I'm showing you the deaths per year in the
U.S. About a half million a year in the United
States, absolutely dwarfs, you know, even alcoho,l and then all of the illicit drugs
you could-- they barely show up on the same screen visually when you compare it to tobacco--
so it's absolutely staggering. And most people who smoke in the U.S. actually
want help, which I mean-- just the unmet clinical need is staggering. So we appreciate as Michael mentioned this
work gets some media attention, we always appreciate that, we wish that was the case
for all science in society, but we're lucky enough to have some in this area. I have to say though we don't have control
over all of the headlines. I'll say you probably got my point now there
are risks, I'm not encouraging anyone to use these things illicitly, but I definitely want
to put special emphasis on that-- this. Don't eat this many mushrooms, that's insane
that's-- that's a lot of mushrooms in that bowl. So definitely don't do that, but definitely
yeah you got me. Demographics. These were first 15 people they were real
deal smokers, the important things were they'd been smoking for an average of over 30 years
and smoking on average about a pack a day. These were not light weights. It was a 15-week protocol where we combined
it with cognitive behavioral therapy. Pretty standard cognitive behavioral therapy
which is the main backdrop for most smoking cessation psychotherapies and three psilocybin
sessions spread out over an eight week period. The first psilocybin session was on their
target quit date. We used two biological measures of success,
cotinine in the urine and carbon monoxide in the breath. Here I'm showing the carbon monoxide results,
this is before psilocybin, and this is after psilocybin. You don't need to be a stats whiz to tell
you probably have a pre-post difference here, and what that equated to in terms of point
prevalence, the percentage of people at any given time at various follow-ups, is that
at six months eighty percent of these folks were biologically confirmed to smoke free. And that held up to sixty percent at an average
of two and a half years. This is just staggering compared-- it's open
label so we can't determine causation, you know, so maybe we were lucky, but in terms
of asking like is this worthy of follow-up, I mean this absolutely dwarfs anything in
the published literature. So is this worthy of follow-up, the answer
was absolutely yes. And I haven't shown these data before, but
something that we're working up now or just a little bit of behavioral economics data--
don't have too much time to explain--, but did the standard cigarette purchasing tasks,
hypothetical purchasing tasks showing demand curves for folks in the study. One of the things that shook out here is that
people who ended up eventually being a treatment success had lower intensity of demand. So probably not surprisingly, you know, it
seemed by this measure appeared to be the harder cases that were less susceptible to
treatment with psilocybin. So hopefully that's like in other areas of
addiction treatment, I think this is one of the useful areas for demand analysis, and
then we also found even amongst the so-called failures, the people that weren't abstinent,
completely abstinent, we did see both in terms of real cigarettes and also what I'm showing
you here a significant reduction in their hypothetical purchasing demand for cigarettes. So nonetheless might be you know signs of
improvement even with the folks who didn't achieve complete abstinence. So back to mystical experience like with cancer,
like with the healthy normals, like with personality change, we found that the degree of mystical
experience had some relationship to long-term success. And this study is ongoing and we're just about
to start it back up very cautiously with extra precautions with the pandemic. But we're most of the way through a randomized
comparative efficacy trial, randomizing 40 folks to psilocybin 40 folks to nicotine patch
treatment with the same cognitive behavioral therapy. And right now for the 44 people who have gotten
through the 12-month follow-up 59% are abstinent. And we only used one-- we scaled back to one
psilocybin session, mainly for experimental reasons. It's too much to get into here, but 59% biologically
confirmed success rate, one year follow-up. So this is still-- results could change, it's
in progress, but this is a number of people most of the way through the study. If I had to have made up data, you know which
I would never do, but if I did I wouldn't make them up, you know this good, because
like no one would believe it. So needless to say we're still very encouraged--
again could change and we're keeping an eye on the data. Just very briefly we are doing in collaboration
with colleagues at the National Institute on Drug Abuse Intramural Research Program,
which is next door to us on our campus. We are doing fMRI imaging there. Ellie stein's lab is donating those resources
because they're so interested in this and these results. We're doing fMRI, and we're also doing some
behavioral and cognitive tasks associated with that. One of the things that's jumped out to us
so far, and a preliminary analysis of some of these people, is improvements in cognitive
control as measured by this MSIT task it's called the oddball task. Basically it's like the Stroop task, you have
to resist a pre-potent urge, and kind of, really kind of-- you're supposed to use the
finger that's not in the spatial location of the target digit, but you're supposed to
select the digit-- the finger number of the oddball digit. So and it's a little confusing with the time
we have, but you have to resist the urge to do something sort of relatively automatic
like a Stroop task. And we found that the people who quit psilocybin
compared to the people who quit the next day, and pretty much everyone was quit the next
day so that's that's not a confound, compared to the people who quit with the nicotine patch
we found substantial improvements in this so-called incongruency effect, and that was
driven by the interference trials. So you had an improvement, a lower, less of
a slowing in reaction time when people were faced with these trials where there was a
conflict between what was automatic and what and what they were supposed to do. So I'm thinking maybe some extrapolation,
but kind of like, what some of our patients tell us they feel like. They get like-- there's a shift-- they say
they deal with cravings in a much more mindful way like. One participant said it was like this, he
says, "now the cravings came out of nowhere, it was like automaticity" we called in the
smoking research. Like their hand ends up in their pocket and
the cigarettes in the mouth automatically, don't have cigarettes you drive into the 7-eleven
automatically. You're on autopilot. Now it's like the craving comes he says: "I'm
like Neo in the movie the matrix, everything's in slow motion, it's like oh there's a cigarette
craving. I quit smoking a week ago. This is gonna pass in a few minutes. No big deal. I got this I can like bend out of the way,
let the bullet go by, say bye bye". So you know maybe some extrapolating there,
but again following the data maybe that's what these data are pointing to. So it's not just smoking our good colleague
Mike Bogenschutz at NYU, he started this work at University of New Mexico, but did a pilot
study with alcohol use disorder with psilocybin. Open label just like ours, and 10 people found
similarly very dramatic reductions. He has now also moved on to a 100 person randomized
clinical trial, which I think he's recently wrapped up so stay tuned. I've also published a number of studies in
our lab, basically documenting these stories of people out there, and there's now thousands
of them that say-- and I started this because people just started reaching out to me when
I'd show our lab data, and they're like this happened to me. And sometimes they said this happened to me
30 years ago or you know five years ago that they had a psychedelic session due to like
experiences because they were partying and they're like what the hell am I doing, they
quit smoking, they quit drinking, and so we've collected these stories. It doesn't show causal effect, but just sort
of to catalog this, and some things have popped out like the nature of the thrall-- too much
to go into-- but we think there's some value there in terms of, you know, getting these
stories down and it's pointed us into some different clinical directions. Like we're now starting an opioid treatment
study, and that was one of the things we studied in this survey work. And we're also it seems like it looks like
we're going to have some funding for cannabis use disorder treatment. There's a number of people said they quit
smoking weed because they had a psychedelic session, which is intriguing. So wrapping up here like: What's really going
on here? So you should be justifiably curious about
a snake oil effect. What are you telling me Matt? This stuff works for everything. I don't think it's gonna work for everything,
but if things hold up it seems to be promising for a number of nominally distinct disorder. What's going on here, is this snake oil? I think the alternative is if there's something
going on here, it's got to be common mechanisms. I think in the spirit of RDoc or you know
transdiagnostic mechanisms, and across disorders, keeping in mind that psychiatry unlike other
areas of medicine is largely descriptive. We really don't know, you know, those different
categories aren't based on a real mechanistic understanding, but on a descriptive understanding. I think this research is pointing us to common
mechanisms. I really think of a number of these disorders
as addiction broadly defined, whether it is the addiction to the behaviors and thoughts,
which I view as just behaviors, associated with a form of a frequent substance use or
whether the thoughts and behaviors associated with depressive disorder, the self-persecutory
thoughts: I'm a failure, no one likes me, I'm just, you know, this is my lot in life,
that type of thing or with anxiety. That all of these are essentially addiction
broadly defined. It's a stuckness, it's a local min in calculus
terms. And once you're in that rut, it's very difficult
to get out of it. And I don't have time to go into it, but some
intriguing work in the area, some of which we've recently replicated, is that these brain
networks, the way that brain areas communicate with each other seems to be a very meaningful
acute outcome. Like these drugs seem to do some very interesting
things in terms of increasing connectivity between brain areas that don't normally communicate,
and combine that with psychiatric research saying a growing number of psychiatric disorders
are associated with abnormal brain network or brain communication signatures, it may
be what we're doing, very crudely I admit it the description at this point, we're shaking
the snow globe up. And somehow we're normalizing these systems
so I think that might just be the biological side, perhaps of what I think we have could
have more confidence in is behaviorally they seem to be behaviorally plastic manipulations,
and that can blast people out of their ruts. And that's-- so perhaps it's also pointing
us to like what the heck are these 2a-- we don't know much about serotonin 2a endogenously. We know it's more expressed in mammals than
other animals, more expressed in the human than others, that are major computational
units in the CNS. I would say it may be that they have a very
important role in modulating what you can call meaning, may be put in another way mental
or behavioral plasticity. So even broader than therapeutics, you know,
like kind of pointing to what's going on with the 2a receptor, and I think about these as
powerful learning experiences, and I think about the simplest kind of classic Rescorla
Wagner learning model. Where in it, you know, there's advancements,
but accounts for a whole lot of experimental data, where you have basically the amount
that's learned on a trial is a product of basically-- the thing learned or the difference
between what you expect and what is novel, you know, and the salience of a trial. You hike up the stimulus light brightness,
and a rat learns more on that trial. Okay now what happens when you embed one of
these treatments, like say quitting smoking into a trial where people come out of it and
say this was one of the most meaningful experiences of my life that I'll never forget. Is that kind of like jacking up the house
light to like a million fold? Just kind of a way to think about this behaviorally
to track. And then another way to say this is that,
Michael Pollan in his book credited me with this dope slap effect, which I wish it sounded
more scientific, but I think it kind of-- I think it sums it up. He says, "I believe that psychedelics can
be used to change all sorts of behaviors.." It's their ability to "sufficiently dope slap
people out of their story, psychedelics open up a window of mental flexibility which people
can let go of the mental models that they organize-- use to organize reality". So again a lot of hand waving there admittedly,

one drugs and it basically takes about anywhere from nine months to a year like you can get
a funder to say I'm going to fund the study for you, and of course you got all the credentials,
and you're at you know research university, you got the positions and everything, but
nonetheless it's about nine months to a year before you jump through all of the hoops. The IRB, the FDA, and the DEA, and there's
an interplay between them, because no one wants to approve anything until everyone else
like approves it. And it's like, believe it or not, there's
no good-- you gotta constantly remind the IRB like "can you basically say you're okay
on everything else until without the FDA approval." Because we don't want to send a protocol to

to change it, and now we have these two like-- it can be maddening. So it's a huge challenge, and we literally

to, and I don't want access to, so we're on, you know, tight security and it's a lot. You know I think in like other areas, there's
I'm well aware that these regulations address extremely important things, and this stuff
should not be in the drinking water, and you know there are risks again. At the same time you look at it and you say,

down on a couch, with the largely introspective you know framework-- Say go within, wear the

with roots in humanistic psychology, the unconditional positive regard and things of this nature. It's like that, you know, that those common
factors I think are just under a magnifying lens here, you know. And-- but it's the same factors. It's like that person really has to know that
they are safe at these various levels because they can think-- the experience can be so
intense that they feel like sometimes it's hard for someone to imagine like I didn't
know it was going to be like this. Like they feel like, just like, they don't
even know what it's like to be a person anymore, and it's like this kind of existential dread,
sometimes having this experience, and this extreme vulnerability and to know just-- I've
been there in over 100 sessions as the guide-- where just to have a human being to hold their
hand and say like "look I'm with you, you know, I'm not leaving you, you're doing okay
just breathe and keep with it, we're here with you. It's gonna be okay" like that just, that cuts
beyond everything else. And again I think that's just a microcosm
that's what we need in just any clinical treatment in some sense. It's just kind of a magnification of that. The sense that you're part of a team, and
that you've got support, and you can let go, and you can be vulnerable. So I think those are the factors that increase
the likelihood of these mystical type experiences because if you have your psychological guards
up it seems like it's not going to happen. >> Dr. Amlung: Okay, we are coming up against

=== VIDEO: X11amMhWkaQ (https://youtu.be/X11amMhWkaQ) ===

Transcriber: Başak Altın
Reviewer: Emma Gon In the 1940s, psychologist 
Abraham Maslow first introduced  what he called the hierarchy 
of needs for human motivation. This is basically a framework
for understanding  how we as humans navigate 
our most fundamental needs. A key to this hierarchy was
that the fulfillment of certain needs is often dependent on
the fulfillment of more basic needs. So at the base of this hierarchy,  you have physiological needs
 like air, food, water.  Next layer up, 
you have physical safety  like shelter, then love 
and belongingness.  And above that 
esteem, including self-esteem. But at the pinnacle of 
Maslow’s original hierarchy was a concept called
 self-actualization, and this refers to one meeting
their full potential as a human engaged in something
 that’s deeply meaningful to them. Maslow put it this way, what a man can be, he must be.  So writers must write,
painters must paint, entrepreneurs must 
build organizations. A key ingredient of self-actualization is the ability to consciously
make free decisions for purposeful action in your life,
scientifically, we can call that agency. To what degree are you a creative force? Do you have an influence in this world? Are you an agent as opposed
 to behaving passively,  falling into habitual behavior? Another aspect of this hierarchy
is that we don’t necessarily follow these different categories 
of needs in a fixed order, and we can address more than
one category at the same time. So, for example, 
it’s possible for someone  to spend a small percentage
 of their motivation  focused on self-actualization  while still addressing 
more fundamental needs. It's also possible that just because
you've met those more basic needs, that you’re not necessarily going
to be focused on self-actualization. So I'll try a little bit
of an experiment here, just based on the description I provided, how many of you 
with a show of hands would say that you spend all of your motivation,  or virtually all of it
focused on self-actualization? I hear a lot of laughs, 
but not a lot of hands.  Okay, second question. How many of you would say  you spend at least 
some of your motivation, even if small, 
focused on self-actualization? Okay, I see some hands popping up there. So this just demonstrates
that we do bounce back and forth  between these different
modes of motivation. For the last 20 years, 
the focus of my scientific career  has been examining the effects 
of psychedelics  in human beings.  What are psychedelics? There are probably 
some suspects that you’ll recognize  psilocybin, the active agent
 in so-called magic mushrooms. LSD probably needs no introduction. Mescaline, the active agent
and the peyote cactus. Dimethyltryptamine, the agent in
the South American sacrament, ayahuasca and MDMA, which is known 
on the street as Molly or ecstasy. During my 20 years of work
with psychedelics, I've come to believe that these
psychedelics constitute a powerful technology  for maximizing
conscious decision making, which could potentially lead
 to increases in self-actualization. Now, psychedelics have a crazy history. In fact, they're pre-historical. There’s evidence that indigenous
cultures throughout the globe have used psychedelics
for thousands of years. But it wasn’t until the 1940s that
they became a real interest in science. And then there was 
a heyday of therapeutic research  with psychedelics
in the 1950s and 1960s.  And during that time, investigators found that LSD
and other psychedelics showed real promise in treating
 terminal cancer patients who were suffering
from existential anxiety, because of their impending death,
as well as treating alcoholism. But despite these early
promising findings, the entire domain of 
human research with psychedelics  was put on the deep freeze
for decades.  And so what was
the reason for this backlash? Well, there were a few.  It’s important to keep in mind
that in the mid to late 60s,  psychedelic use became pretty
 popular amongst young people.  And amongst
 that large number of people, there were some real casualties, including people with 
psychiatric vulnerabilities,  people used it reckelessly
and in unsafe situations. But probably even bigger than
that as a cause for the backlash  is this association between
psychedelics and the counterculture. The cultural influence 
of psychedelics was pervasive, and music from everyone 
from the Beatles to Jimi Hendrix, and psychedelics were associated with so many of the social
 movements of the day, such as the anti-Vietnam War protests. And these things scared
a lot of segments of society. Reinforcing that backlash it was
the public discovered in the 1970s that the CIA, under their project MKUltra, had been conducting clandestine
research with psychedelics, including dosing of American citizens
without their knowledge or consent. But about 20 years ago,
around the time when I started doing
 work with psychedelics, we stepped into an era 
that’s now come to be known as the renaissance of 
human psychedelic research. And this started out with 
a small number of laboratories  around the planet
 that at first conducted just a very few studies
and a tiny number of people.  Fast forward to today, 
things have radically changed. It's probably easier for me to count the number of major
 medical institutions  without a psychedelic
 research program than it is to count
the number of such institutions  with a psychedelic 
research program. There is finally some limited
amount of federal funding  for therapeutic research
 with psychedelics. In fact, I was very lucky. A few years ago, I received
 the first federal funding  from the US government
and about a half century  for therapeutic research with 
some of these classic psychedelics,  specifically my work using psilocybin to see if we can treat tobacco addiction. There’s also 
a multi-billion dollar industry  now surrounding these psychedelics,  with a number of companies
moving psychedelics along the FDA pathway looking towards
 potential medical approval as medicines. But all of that progress is because of
a growing number of clinical trials  that have demonstrated that psychedelics
often have high rates of efficacy in treating some intractable disorders including cancer distress, 
major depressive disorder, anxiety, bipolar two disorder, PTSD, and multiple forms of addiction. Now, psychedelic therapy flies in the face of your standard 
psychiatric treatment paradigm. Rather than taking a pill every day
in order to treat symptoms, patients are administered 
one, 2 or 3 doses of the psychedelic, under careful, monitored supervision. And yet, we’re seeing 
improvements in addictive behavior, depression and PTSD, months 
and even in some cases, years later. Twenty years ago, the frontier
 of this psychedelic renaissance was determining
 whether these psychedelics  could potentially treat 
some of these psychiatric disorders.  Now, thankfully,
there is countless academic groups and companies that are pursuing
that medical pathway. That’s important work
and it’s going to continue. But I’m arguing today
that the frontier has moved on. We're missing an elephant in the room. The burning question is,
why in the world are these psychedelics and these experiences, 
why are they able to treat  such nominally distinct disorders?  What’s the commonality? And the biggest thing
 that jumps out to me  and where I think the frontier is at,  I believe that psychedelics can maximize conscious,
free decision making, allowing us to step into
that mode of self-actualization. And that’s at play in
all of these disorders. When people are healed 
with psychedelics, something really shifts inside of them. I’m going to tell you 
about a patient experience 15 years ago, or so, I was with a patient 
that was experiencing a high dose psilocybin session. And he said he had the insight that, and this was part of a study,  to see if psilocybin could
help with his tobacco addiction. He said he had the insight
 that his addiction to cigarettes was like a small bug 
that had landed on him.  And he said he could 
just flick it off, easily. And he said, I know this sounds stupid, but I can just decide to quit smoking. I can decide to quit smoking.  And I’ve come to call 
these “duh experiences”. And you see quite a bit of these 
in these psychedelic sessions, you know. Okay, you know. 
Yeah, you can quit smoking. You’ve decided to quit smoking
before and it hasn’t worked out. The reason I call them 
“duh” experiences  is because the people
themselves know  that it sounds trite,
 that it sounds hollow, but they’re feeling something profound, that they’re not able
to fully express in words. In fact, we even have a fancy
scientific term for that. We say that these psychedelic 
experiences can be ineffable. It's when the person says that no matter
how hard they try to describe the magnitude of this  experience, 
they can’t come anywhere close to fully capturing it. But even though these experiences
are often ineffable, we as scientists kind of like
to try to “f” that up by asking the patients to do their best
 to describe the experiences.  But the -- an important point about this
is that this wasn’t a fleeting insight, he felt just as strongly about this insight in the days and the weeks following the session.  And that helped him quit smoking.  When people are successfully treated  for these various disorders
with psychedelics, they change their belief in themselves. There’s an increase in their belief that they can change
 their own behavior and thoughts. Whether it’s abandoning
a decades old addiction or whether that’s changing the way  you’ve come to think about yourself in the context of depression, or changing your relationships to your
own memories in the context of PTSD. Ultimately, I think what 
we’re seeing is the potential for large scale behavior change, enabled by a sense of agency, a sense that one has the ability
to change their own behavior, an increase in the sense of free will. And I think we all intuitively appreciate the role of conscious
decision making in our lives, regardless of psychedelics.  And think about those times
 when you’re inspired. You feel like a creative force in life.
You have some ability to influence. And I’d argue many of
 our greatest challenges in society  are related to
conscious decision making, not just the mental health crisis
and health generally, social media, 
our relationships to each other, our relationships to the environment,
conflict resolution. So I think the challenge is, how can we dive into
this science of psychedelics and their potential to increase
conscious decision making and allow us to step into
that mode of self-actualization? What would that science look like? Well, there are some ideas that you can
draw from psychology and neuroscience. One example is we know that
we can use brain waves to detect when a person is going 
to make a voluntary action, even before that person
 is consciously aware  that they’re going
 to initiate that action.  And that finding
 has been discussed for years   in the context of the philosophical 
debate over free will. But what I’m interested in
is what would a psychedelic do to the timing of that awareness? There’s also a phenomenon 
surrounding voluntary action  where subjective time
warps surrounding voluntary action. In other words, 
people’s estimation of the time  between an action that they take and a consequence from
 the environment tends to contract. It's a way that we psychologically
bind our actions and their consequences
in our perceptions of the world. How would a psychedelic experience, 
and its aftermath affect that? In another line of research, instead of focusing on the treatment
of particular psychiatric disorders, what about examining the effects
 of psychedelics on individuals  who bring their own idiosyncratic 
behavior change goals to the study?  And that way, we can
start to examine psychedelics as a generalized behavior
 change technology. I think these lines of research
can help us bridge the gap between these intriguing findings
in treating mental health disorders, and a line of research
that can really extend us into optimizing self-actualization. Unfortunately, this research agenda is not
one that, understandably, companies developing psychedelics as medications are
 immediately interested in. Nor is it an idea that lends itself
towards government funding agencies, which tend to shy away
from bold ideas. But I think despite these funding
challenges to engage in this work, it’s critical that we do
this type of work by leaning into this work to understand how psychedelics really affect
our conscious decision making. We really have an opportunity to learn
about and to maximize human flourishing. Thank you. (Applause)

ethnobotanist and psychonaut Terrence McKenna McKenna coined this phrase to describe a dose of five dried grams of psybin mushrooms which he believed was the threshold needed to experience the profound life-altering States Of Consciousness that psychedelics are known for but the concept of of a heroic dose goes beyond just the quantity it's about the courage and willingness to delve deep bring up challenging experiences and emotions and should therefore be approached with caution in a nutshell a heroic dose is a powerful tool for personal growth and healing but it's not to be taken lightly it's a journey into the deepest corners of the Mind a voyage into the unknown but how does this heroic dose translate in a scientific setting science and psychedelics might seem like two the depths of the human mind and the effects of substances like syoc cybin the active ingredient found in magic mushrooms Dr Johnson's research has been revealing insights that challenge the conventional understanding of psychedelics through carefully controlled and meticulously designed studies his team has been able to quantify and analyze the effects of what's known as a heroic dose of siloc cybin in this context a heroic dose isn't about bravado or recklessness but rather a reference to a specific amount that's believed to trigger profound and potentially therapeutic psychological experiences the science behind this concept is intriguing when someone ingests a heroic dose of psilocybin the substance interacts with the serotonin receptors in the brain this can lead to a temporary disruption of normal brain function creating the unique and often Indescribable experiences associated with a psychedelic trip but it's not just about the trip the potential for lasting positive change is what really gets the scientific Community excited Studies have shown that a single dose of psilocybin administered in a controlled setting can lead to significant improvements in measures of depression and anxiety changes that persist for months or even years after the experience it's a promising area of research offering a potential new Avenue for treating mental health unpredictable varying greatly from person to person based on factors like mental health physical health and environment Dr Johnson emphasizes that the potential benefits of a heroic dose should not be drug effects have worn off, we ask people first in
a free recall sense, can you just tell me all
the words you remember? Then we challenge them
with a list of words, some of which they had been presented before and
some of which haven't. In terms of the recognition, this receiver operator
characteristic curve has a [inaudible]
area under the curve when you are less
accurate at recalling or recognizing words that
were shown to you before. The take home here is there are no effective psilocybin on
episodic memory performance, but there is an effective extra
methorphan on memory performance. So far we've covered, very briefly, perception and some
dissociable effects of psilocybin on various
cognitive processes. Some really important and maybe the most peculiar
and most fascinating of subjective effects
and acute effects of psychedel drugs may most generally
described as peak experiences. These are experiences for which
the field has struggled to come up with really adequate language
and good operationalizations. Some have called these
experiences, ego disillusion. We're thoroughly used a model called mystical experiences to describe what people can encounter during high
doses of psilocybin. The concept of a mystical experience harkens back at least to
the work of William James, first described in his varieties of religious experience in the
early 1900s and picked up and further developed
by Walter Stace in 1960 with a book on
mystical experiences. Stace was a philosopher of religion and a philosopher
of psychology, and he went through all of the texts that he
could find in the world. All of the recorded texts that described something that seemed
like a mystical experience, in a dense and expansive
qualitative analysis of those descriptions. Came up with a model in which a
mystical experience consistent of a feeling of unity or oneness;
oneness with your self, oneness with the world around you, oneness with the universe, really this unity is best characterized as a breakdown of
boundary between self and other. Also, other descriptive
aspects of the experience, such as a feeling of sacredness, a feeling of noetic quality, or there was something
at a ground-level, purely true about the experience
and deeply felt positive mood. Transcendence of time and space such that the experience of time and space really began to disintegrate
or broke down completely. Enough ability or the inability to really describe
these things in words. When we try to quantify mystical experiences during and after a second
administration sessions, we don't ask people, did you
have a mystical experience? Rather, we give them
a questionnaire, what psychologists would I be if
I didn't have a questionnaire? >> We gave people a
questionnaire in which they rate each of these
dimensions of experience. To what degree did you feel at
one with the world around you? To what degree did you feel
joy, or ecstatic experiences? Rate on a scale 1-5, how much the experience of
time seem to dissipate, or things like this, and
from this we can quantify some degree of self-reported
mystical experience. These are all
subjective or cognitive level behavioral descriptions, and models, and domains of
experience that we have studied. There are also a number of circuit level models in
the brain that have been proposed to try to explain how and why some of
these things are happening. I'm not going to go into
depth with any of these, we could spend an entire hour
talking about one of these. But briefly, there's
the cortico-striatum of sound cortical circuit model, this is a model that's
been implicated in various aspects of psychosis, and has also now been really well studied to some degree to try to explain the acute
effects of sect of drugs. In this model, the thalamus
is a bit of a sensory gate, and the thalamus is a
structure responsible for passing sensory information
onto the cortex for processing. The cortex provides
some top-down input, or top-down feedback to the
thalamus and the striatum in order to moderate the sensory
information coming forward. There are really
bidirectional connections between the cortex and the thalamus to manage the passage
of sensory information. With the onset of a
psychedelic experience, but really with the administration
of psychedelic drug there are two way receptor sites along this entire cortical striatum
thalamus cortical circuit. It's not exactly clear
where this begins, but the model describes
an effect by which psychedelic drug administration
and really serotonin A receptor signaling begins to
disrupt this circuit. Maybe first disrupting
thalamocortical thalamic gating to the cortex such that a greater flow of sensory information goes
from thalamus to cortex. That can influx in cortical
sensory information begins to reduce the ability of the cortex to provide top-down
filtering information, which then turns into a
positive feedback loop. The cortex becomes overwhelming
sensory information, and then everything else happens. This maybe a good model to describe sensory disturbances as
well as breakdown of cognitive control during psychedelic
experiences that may not describe everything with
psychedelic experiences. Another model we're working on is claustro-cortical model
where the claustro which is a deeply seated thin strip of cortex in the center of
each of the hemispheres. The claustro we believe maybe
responsible for the instantiation of cortical networks in response to
task demands in the environment. Really the claustro itself may act
as a bit of a meeting planner, or a switchboard, or something of this nature to help really shift network states to respond to changing demands both
internally and externally. Claustro is also one of the
brain regions that most densely expresses
serotonin receptors. It is of great interest

the conductor of consciousness, the claustro is one of the most
densely interconnected regions to all areas of cortex. If there were one brain region that we're responsible
for seamlessly stitching together our
continuous percept of the world and in

for processes and actions, then the claustro would

is just about everything. It turns out the claustro

if it was stitching everything together

separate sense in which it directs transitions between different brain states as we are continuing to go about the
world doing human things. With the washing on psychedelic drug or increasing serotonin 2A receptor
signaling in the claustro, we believe that reduces

between network states. We believe that maybe
another explanation of why people experience such vastly different

the inner play between these regions is involved in responding to predictions about the world psychedelics may
break down that model. For this, I would call a

psychedelics do acutely, well, they lead to
changes in perception. They lead to selective impairment

all of these things may have varying circuit level

do post acutely, and I think this is one of the

across a number of indications. So far the evidence is beginning to amass behind cancer-related
anxiety and stress, which may also be
adjustment disorder. While we have major
depressive disorder and treatment resistant depression. We also have studies

disorder, alcohol use disorder, and studies ongoing with
cocaine use disorder, opioid use disorder,
cannabis use disorder, and the list is growing. The first clinical finding out of our lab at Hopkins was
published in 2016. This was one of the
largest clinical trials at the time to study the effects of psilocybin in the
clinical population. The question was whether we could use psilocybin to help
reduce depression, and anxiety secondary to
late stage cancer diagnosis. If you've ever known
someone who has had cancer or late-stage
cancer diagnosis, or yourself have encountered one, you may know that this
existential crisis maybe one of the most difficult things to treat
aside from the actual cancer. In this study, 50 patients
were randomized to receive either a high
dose of psilocybin in their first session or an incredible low trivial dose of psilocybin, a non psychoactive
dose of psilocybin, in their first therapeutic session. After a couple of weeks, participants were crossed over to receive condition
they hadn't received. On the left you see two plots. One is the GRID-HAMD for depression, the Hamilton depression
rating scale, and the Hamilton
anxiety rating scale. On the y-axis you see severity
of depression or anxiety, and on the x-axis you see time. From baseline right after
the first treatment in red, we see the effect of psilocybin on the individuals who received very
high doses of psilocybin first. A precipitous drop into both
depression and anxiety, into remitted stages that
persisted to at least six months. Very interestingly,
the blue line depicts those who received a low
dose of psilocybin first, trivial non psychoactive ghosts. They show about half of the effect that those who
received high dose showed. I would argue that this is one of the first really clear demonstration of the extra pharmacological
effects of this intervention. A lot goes into these sessions
with preparation, guiding, and facilitation
during the experience, and follow up and aftercare. That all has substantial
therapeutic value, and I believe this is a good
depiction of that therapeutic value. It is not really just a drug in
healing out type of approach. That this is really pharmaco
psychotherapy approach. Whereas the red line, I think, depicts the effect of
the overall package and the blue line depicts the
extra pharmacological effects, everything about the drug. As soon as we administer high doses psilocybin to those
who received the low dose first, they come right down to where high-dose first folks
were and stay there. These effects were republished
in 2016 at about the same time our colleagues at

most folks from a moderate to severe depression

after about three months. This was in 2016. In 2020 we published the results of a weightless
controlled trial in patients with major

12 months after treatment, over 50 percent of the

to one year post psilocybin. These were 24 individuals, it was still a small sample. On the left side you

depression that then popped right backup to pre-treatment levels at about three months post-session. Then, yet still there are at least two conspicuous
individuals the most conspicuous of which is the individual

through the center of the graph. What we can say here is that
for some folks in this sample, there seemed to be rapid and enduring therapeutic
effect of psilocybin. For some folks, there was a less durable effect and for some people they
were non-responders. >> Following that last year, our colleagues in London

with substance use disorder. There were thousands of studies on psychedelic drugs that were
published in the '50s and '60s. The vast majority of
which do not really meet modern criteria for rigor
and reproducibility in science. In 2012, Krebs and
Johansen published the results of a literature
research in meta-analysis where they identified a sum total of six studies in which
they felt there were adequate controls in the study of LSD as a treatment for
alcohol use disorder. Upon analyzing these data they
found favorable effect of LSD over the control conditions
that were used in these studies, variously placebo or some other active drug condition
or treatment as usual. These all hang together
and comport with Humphry Osmond's claim that between 1954 and 1960 he
treated over 2,000 patients, and he claimed an abstinence rate of 40-45 percent abstinence at
one year post treatment. Again, that hadn't
been peer reviewed or published but this is
his clinical report. Much more recently, our
colleague here Matt Johnson, in the Department of Psychiatry, in 2014 published the first-ever
study of psilocybin to treat patients with tobacco use disorder in a 15 person open
label pilot study. He basically showed that after treatment folks who had been
smoking a substantial amount were biologically verified
abstinence smoking with exhaled carbon monoxide
and urinary cotinine. For not only six months
follow-up but this LT, long term is at least a year

with psilocybin remained abstinent for over a year smoking and that completely blows
the standard of care, maybe the gold standard treatment
vertically into the patch you would expect about 30-35 percent

over a year later. Matt now just about completed his randomized controlled trial

of Psychiatry, conducted a study

spontaneously stopped quitting after having a

tells us all sorts of things. One of the questions they asked

were after their psilocybin assisted quit success compared

compared to previous attempts. It was actually affective components of withdrawal

previous quit attempts. This gave us a lot of room

post treatment time period. I saw a similar reduction in amygdala reactivity in patients
treated for depression. In our wait-list control trial, we showed not only a reduction in amygdala reactivity to
negative emotional stimuli a week after psilocybin treatment, but a correlation of
that amygdala reduction with therapeutic effects at least up to three
months post psilocybin. This is the beginning of my argument that negative affect maybe a
trans diagnostic process that is targeted by psilocybin and may be a core component
not only of depression, but also of substance use disorders especially to the degree
that negative affect is a core component of
craving withdraw that leads to the development
and maintenance of substance use disorders. >> We spoke a bit about mystical

ANDREW HUBERMAN: Welcome
to the Huberman Lab podcast where we discuss science
and science-based tools for everyday life. [MUSIC PLAYING] I'm Andrew Huberman,
and I'm a professor of neurobiology
and ophthalmology at Stanford School of Medicine. Today, we are
discussing psilocybin. Psilocybin is a psychedelic,
meaning it modifies the psyche. And in doing so, it changes
our level of consciousness. Psychedelics, such as
psilocybin changed the way that we perceive the outside
world and our internal world, our memories, our thoughts,
our feelings, et cetera. Not just while one is under
the influence of psilocybin, but it can also
fundamentally change all of those things afterwards
and for a very long period of time afterwards
as well, which is one of the
reasons why there's growing excitement about the
application of psilocybin and other psychedelics
for the treatment of various mental health
issues, such as depression, alcohol abuse disorder, and
addictions of various kinds, as well as things like
OCD and eating disorders. Today, we will
discuss psilocybin, talking about what it is. In fact, you may be surprised to
learn that psilocybin basically is serotonin. Now, for those of you that
are familiar with psilocybin and serotonin, you might
think, wait, that's not true. But, in fact, psilocybin main
effect is to mimic serotonin. But it does it in
a very specific way because it activates a subset
of serotonin receptors in a very strong fashion leading
to neuroplasticity at the level of the neural
circuits, that is the brain areas and the connections
that serve things like memory and perception. So if any of that is
confusing at this point, I promise to make it all
clear in just a few minutes. Psilocybin is one of many
psychedelics, of course. There are things like
LSD, DMT, 5-MeO-DMT. Even MDMA while not considered
a classic psychedelic is considered a psychedelic
in the general sense. Today's episode is going
to focus on psilocybin in particular. I will tell you

changes brain circuitry. I'll talk about the
clinical effects what's been demonstrated in

dosages and translating from psilocybin mushrooms
to actual psilocybin, and the compound
that actually exerts the effects of psilocybin which
it turns out is not psilocybin, but something called psilocin. Psilocin is the
actual compound that goes into the brain to
create all the changes in consciousness and
all the rewiring effects that we associate
with psilocybin. So understanding how psilocybin
is converted to psilocin has tremendous impact on
the duration of a psilocybin journey, whether or not
that psilocybin journey is going to lead to a
short or longer window for neuroplasticity. In fact, many people
don't realize this. But much of the positive changes

a little bit about chemistry. But I promise to make

about some cell biology, the actual

into their past, their present, and their future,

positive way from things that formerly were depressing

to talk quite a lot about the conditions inside
of a psilocybin journey that make it actually

that I'll make several times throughout today's

something invokes neuroplasticity changes
in brain circuitry does not mean that it's
therapeutic, or I should say, does not necessarily mean
that it's therapeutic. For neuroplasticity
to be therapeutic, it has to be adaptive,
it has to allow someone to function better in life
than they did previously. So today, we will talk about how
the conditions of a psilocybin journey, including
whether or not it's done with eyes

particular journeys, the structure of the

eyes open or eyes closed, the particular

going to focus specifically on psilocybin. And we're going to set aside
all the other psychedelics for future episodes. Psilocybin is what's
called a tryptamine. That refers to its
chemical composition, not to the so-called
psychedelic trip. In fact, it's
spelled differently. Tryptamine is T-R-Y-P.
Trip, T-R-I-P, of course. Tryptamines include
psilocybin, but also things like DMT and 5-MeO-DMT. The tryptamine
psychedelics very closely resemble serotonin itself. That's right. Most of you have probably heard
of the chemical serotonin. And serotonin is what's
called a neuromodulator, which means your brain and
body naturally make it, and that it modifies or changes
the activity of other neurons and neural circuits. And it does that generally by
either increasing or decreasing the activity of those
neural circuits. If I were to show you a picture
of the chemical structure of psilocybin or its
active derivative psilocin, and I were to also
put right alongside it an image of the chemical
structure of serotonin, provided that you weren't
a chemist who really likes to focus on the detailed
differences between things, you would say those
look very similar. And indeed, psilocybin in
its active form psilocin are very similar
structurally and chemically to serotonin itself. Now, as I mentioned before,
serotonin is something that you naturally make. And yes, it's true
that about 90% of the serotonin in
your brain and body is manufactured in your gut. However, contrary
to popular belief, the serotonin in your
brain is not manufactured from the serotonin in your gut. You have separate independent
sources of serotonin. That is you have
particular neurons that make serotonin in your brain. You also have
serotonin in your gut and those work more or less
in parallel separately. Now, what does serotonin do. This is really important
to understand because of the similarity
between psilocybin in its active form
psilocin and serotonin. Serotonin in that
it's a neuromodulator changes the activity
of other neurons and the net effects
of those changes are things that
you're familiar with. For instance, satiety or
the feeling that we've had enough of various
things, such as food, or a social interaction, or
sex, or pleasure of any kind. Serotonin is involved
in all of that. And an enormous number
of other things, such as mood regulation such
as our sense of pleasure itself, or lack of
pleasure, such as whether or not we feel motivated
or not motivated. It works in concert with
other neuromodulators, such as dopamine and
epinephrine and norepinephrine. In fact, if this were an
episode about serotonin, which it is not. You would soon
realize that serotonin is involved in so many
different functions that impact our daily life. And that is one reason
why certain antidepressant medications which alter
either increase or decrease the amount of serotonin
transmission in the brain will often have a
lot of side effects related to things like mood,
libido, appetite, sleep, et cetera. It's because
serotonin is involved in so many different things. And serotonin is involved
in so many different things because there are a lot of
different so-called serotonin receptors. Serotonin is a chemical
that we call a ligand. And the chemical
ligand is simply the thing that plugs into the
receptor for that chemical or ligand. The receptors, in this
case, serotonin receptors, have the opportunity to do
all sorts of different things. They can change the activity
of neurons making them more active or less active. They can cause growth factors
to be released making sure that those neurons
reinforce or even build up stronger connections
so that they're more likely to be
active in the future. Serotonin binding to
particular receptors can even change the gene
expression in particular cells making those cells proliferate. So make more of them. Making those cells more
robust, making those cells interact with new elements
of the brain and body. Basically, serotonin and all
these different receptors that it binds to has dozens,
if not hundreds, and maybe even thousands of
different functions. So the fact that psilocybin
so closely resembles serotonin leads to a very
important question that we should all be asking
ourselves, which is, why is it that psilocybin, which looks
so much like serotonin when one takes it in the form
of magic mushrooms, or some other form, maybe the
synthetic form of psilocybin itself, which nowadays is
manufactured in laboratories and placed in different
psilocybin containing foods and pills, et cetera. Why that leads to complex
yet fairly circumscribed sets of experience like visual
and auditory hallucinations, changes in particular
thought patterns, and neuroplasticity
that, in many cases, in the clinical setting provided
things are done correctly. Improvements in mood,
relief from depression, relief from various compulsive
disorders, et cetera. All right. This is really what
you need to understand if you want to understand
psilocybin, and how it works, and how to make
it work optimally for a given condition or goal. You have to understand
what it's actually doing and what allows psilocybin
to do fairly specific things in comparison to serotonin. Even though psilocybin and
serotonin are so similar is that psilocybin mainly
binds to and activates the so-called serotonin
to a receptor. The serotonin to
receptors is one of, again, many different
serotonin receptors. But serotonin 2A is expressed
in particular areas of the brain and even on particular areas
of neurons in the brain that allow for very specific types
of changes in neural circuitry to take place, not
just when one is under the influence
of psilocybin, but afterwards as well. So really in order to
have a useful discussion about psilocybin, we need to
talk a lot about the serotonin to a receptor. But fortunately for
you, unless you're somebody really interested
in structural biology or cell biology, that
discussion is not going to be about the binding pocket
for serotonin on serotonin 2A receptor, or a lot of
the downstream signaling of the serotonin to a receptor. We'll talk a little bit about
that where it's relevant. But more importantly, at least
for sake of today's discussion, we're going to talk about
how the serotonin 2A receptors really the one
responsible for triggering all the changes in
neural circuitry that lead to the changes, that
is the improvements in mood, the relief from compulsive
disorders in many cases, but really it's the serotonin
2A receptor selectivity of psilocybin that is
leading to all the excitement that you hear about
in terms of psilocybin as a therapeutic tool. Let me say that from a
slightly different angle. There are data that I'll
talk about today, which show that one,
although in most cases, two psilocybin journeys
done with particular dosages of psilocybin lead
to maximal binding or occupancy of those
serotonin 2A receptors in ways that lead to significant
and unprecedented relief for major depression. In fact, you'll soon learn
that the clinical trials for psilocybin are
outperforming standard therapy and outperforming
so-called SSRIs and various other
antidepressants in terms of providing depression
relief in ways that are frankly staggering not just to me, but
to the psychiatric community at large. And this is where so much of
the excitement is coming from. Now that statement
could be taken one way, which is to
just say, OK, well, here's a compound psilocybin
that outperforms SSRIs. And, therefore,
all the attention should be on psilocybin. But SSRI stands for Selective
Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitor. In other words,
the SSRIs of which there is now a lot
of controversy things like Prozac, Zoloft, et cetera-- I'm sure you've heard
some of this controversy. There are people who are
very pro SSRIs although there are a growing number of
people who really feel that the SSRIs are probably
most appropriate for things like obsessive compulsive
disorder where they, in fact, can be very beneficial. But there is a lot of
leaning back from SSRIs as the be all end all for
the treatment of depression nowadays because of the
side effect profiles. And the fact that it's
not even really clear that serotonin deficiencies are
the major cause of depression in the first place. Now, again, we're talking about
psilocybin not about SSRIs, but you should be
thinking, wait, how is it that two
molecules psilocybin and some particular
SSRI both of which look like and/or
increase serotonin transmission in the brain are
leading to either incredibly positive and
interesting outcomes or to troubling side
effect riddled outcomes? And, again, it all
boils back down to the selectivity
of psilocybin to bind that serotonin to a receptor. And so in order to understand
how psilocybin works and in order to understand
proper dosing profiles and spacing of sessions
a.k.a. journeys, we really need to talk a little
bit more about the serotonin to a receptor, where it is in
the brain, what sorts of things happen when psilocybin binds
the serotonin to a receptor, and how those things
set in motion, the various changes,
the neuroplasticity that allows people to feel

psilocybin in order to obtain. So before going
any further, I just want to place an
image in your mind. You can place an image in your
mind whereby when serotonin is released in the
brain naturally not having taken any
compound, any drug, anything. It's getting released a
lot of different sites binding to a lot of different
serotonin receptors, doing a lot of different things. When somebody takes an
SSRI, the net effect of that selective serotonin
reuptake inhibitor is that there's more serotonin
around to exert its effects. Because it's a
reuptake inhibitor at the synapse, the
connections between neurons, the serotonin can do its
thing more extensively and for longer periods of time. But it's doing it
non-specifically. So when you think about standard
antidepressant treatments, at least for sake
of this discussion, you think of a sprinkling
or a spraying of serotonin at different locations
in the brain and binding to lots of different receptors. Whereas when you think
about psilocybin, even though the subjective

molecule of psilocybin that looks a lot
like serotonin that is selectively and very
strongly binding to and activating that
serotonin to a receptor. So that's the image I'd like
you to embed in your mind. And then the next image I'd
like you to embed in your mind is where these
serotonin 2A receptors are located in the brain. Now, the serotonin 2A
receptors are located in multiple brain regions. But they have a tremendous
amount of expression in the so-called neocortex,
the outside of the brain that includes things like our
prefrontal cortex, which is involved in understanding
context, which behaviors, thoughts, and
speech patterns are appropriate for
certain circumstances, how to switch context and
category switch when you go from playing sports, to
hanging out with friends, to being in a
professional setting. You change your behavior
in the way that you speak and perhaps even the
way that you think. You might think some things
that are out of context. But you probably keep
those to yourself. And your ability to
keep those to yourself are dependent on a
functional prefrontal cortex. There are a lot of 5-HT2A. And by the way 5-HT is the
abbreviation for serotonin. So there are a lot of
serotonin 2 receptors in the prefrontal cortex,
also in other areas of the cortex that
are associated with sensation and perception. That is hearing of sounds, that
is seeing of particular things. And in particular, there
is a very, very, very high expression of serotonin 2A
receptors in the visual cortex. And that is one of the
reasons why psilocybin triggers visual hallucinations. And provided
psilocybin is present at sufficient enough
concentration that is taken at a
sufficient dosage, one will experience profound visual
hallucinations regardless of whether or not their eyes are
open or their eyes are closed. Now, that's an important
fact because it explains one of the major
effects of psilocybin that people experience
while they are on the drug. Now, as I'll talk about
a little bit later in terms of what constitutes
a useful psilocybin session, useful meaning that
it's leading to adaptive improvements in mood, adaptive
improvements in creativity, in cognition, et cetera, is that
people not have their eyes open for at least the majority
of the psilocybin session, this is something I've discussed
with several experts who are running clinical
studies on psilocybin in their laboratories,
some of whom are going to be guests
on the Huberman Lab podcast in upcoming episodes, Again, I can't
underscore this enough. Because your visual
cortex contains so many of these
serotonin 2A receptors, and because psilocybin binds
so strongly to that serotonin to a receptor, you're
going to experience a lot of visual
hallucinations when you are under the
influence of psilocybin. There's no surprise there. This has been known
for hundreds, if not, thousands of years. It's one of the main reasons
why people take psilocybin. However, as I mentioned
earlier, these hallucinations occur even when the
eyes are closed. And it's now fairly
well-established that if people are to
take psilocybin and have their eyes open, much
of their cognition, much of their thinking,
much of the time spent in that psilocybin
journey is focused on the altered
perceptions of things in the outside environment. Sometimes this looks like a
fracturing of the outside world into geometric shapes. Sometimes it
appears as a melting of things in the
visual environment, including people's faces or
a morphing of people's faces. All of that has a
strong let's just call it a draw for
a lot of people who are looking for a
highly unusual experience inside of the
psilocybin journey. But I think if one's goal is
to derive long-lasting benefit from the psilocybin
experience, it's very clear that having an eye
mask or some other eye covering or something that ensures
that one's eyes are closed for the majority, if not, the
entire psilocybin session is going to be very
useful because it's going to limit the extent
to which one is focused on those outside changes
in visual perception a.k.a. hallucinations and rather
will allow the person to go inward to combine
whatever it is that they happen to be seeing in their mind's
eye with the different thoughts and memories and changes
in their emotions that are occurring. And that going inward by
staying in the eye mask, at least for the
majority of the time, seems to be a very, if
not, the critical feature of making the psilocybin
journey effective in the therapeutic sense. Now once again, I want to cue to
some of the safety precautions here. I'm going to say this at least

are able to tightly control the amount of psilocybin. And the typical dosage that's
used in clinical studies ranges from 1 milligram
often given repeatedly from day-to-day over
long periods of time, so-called microdosing. And really that 1 milligram
per day or even up to 3 milligrams per day
repeatedly over time is what people generally
think of as microdosing. As compared to say a
10-milligram dose given once, maybe twice in two separate
sessions, or a 25 to 30 milligram dosage that's
given once or twice. Now, those amounts of 1 to 3
milligrams or 10 milligrams, or 25 to 30 milligrams
might not mean much to those of
you that don't think about these things in
the research terms. Perhaps, you've
heard of microdosing and you've also heard of macro
or quote-unquote heroic dosing. OK. That's common or I should
say popular nomenclature for psychedelics. And I'll circle back to
that in a few minutes. But I think one of the
questions that I hear a lot is, how much psilocybin is present
in a given amount of mushrooms? And so the way this
typically works is that mushrooms are often
discussed in terms of grams, or ounces. So an 1/8 of mushrooms
refers to an 1/8 of an ounce of mushrooms, or x
number of grams of mushrooms. The breakdown is
actually quite simple. 1000 milligrams equals 1 gram. And the concentration
of psilocybin in most so-called magic
mushrooms is about 1%. So 1 gram of mushrooms
being 1000 milligrams means that it contains
approximately. And, again, it's approximately
10 milligrams of psilocybin. And in most of the
clinical studies, it's been shown that the
dosage of 25 to 30mg given-- or I should say,
taken once or twice-- and we'll talk about the spacing
of sessions a little bit later, taken once or twice is what's
leading to the most pronounced therapeutic outcomes. But, of course, with enhanced
therapeutic outcomes, one also observes enhanced
side effect profiles or what are called adverse events. So there's an important
nuanced conversation that has to take place. But right now, we're talking
about the conversion of grams of mushrooms to psilocybin. So 1 gram of mushrooms being
1000 milligrams containing 1% psilocybin means
that it contains 10 milligrams of psilocybin. Now, the so-called heroic
doses that you've heard about. And this is something
that's discussed. More with the let's call
them traditional or classic Psychonauts. These are people that may
have an advanced degree, but typically are not
running laboratories exploring the
effects of psilocybin in controlled clinical trials. These are people who have been
long time explorers, and often writers, and people
who have been spokespeople for psilocybin
and other psychedelics. And they will often refer to
the so-called heroic doses. It's a little bit
hard to translate from that informal community
to the scientific data. But in discussing that topic
with various researchers who run laboratories at
major universities focused on psychedelic
therapies, what I was told is that the quote-unquote
heroic dose that's often discussed really
refers to a 5-gram or so dose of mushrooms. So what that translates to is
50 milligrams of psilocybin. So when you hear someone talk
about a quote-unquote heroic dose, they're probably
referring to ingestion of 50 milligrams or
so of psilocybin, but in its mushroom form. So about 5 grams of mushrooms. And, again, it's
important to point out that the concentration
of psilocybin in different
strains of mushrooms and in different
batches, and depending on the age of those mushrooms,
and how they've been stored, et cetera, can vary tremendously
from batch to batch. In fact, there are
some laboratories that have explored the range
of psilocybin concentration in different mushroom strains
and different so-called magic mushrooms. And that range is pretty broad. It's anywhere from 1/2%
all the way up to 2%. What that means is
that someone might get a hold of 1 gram of
mushrooms thinking that they're taking 10 milligrams
of psilocybin in those mushrooms when,
in fact, they're actually taking 20. Or somebody could take 3
grams of mushrooms thinking they're taking 30
grams of psilocybin and in fact they're
only taking 10 or 15 milligrams of psilocybin. So the sourcing is
really key obviously as things become more legal, and
more regulated, and more used in the therapeutic setting. And this is what's
happening more and more. Or as people start to rely on
synthetically made psilocybin as opposed to using mushrooms
to ingest psilocybin. Then certainly,
the dosage thing is going to be more consistent
from batch to batch because we're not talking
about batches of mushrooms, we're talking about batches
of psilocybin itself. So now, I'd like
to take a step back from all this chemistry
and cell biology and talk a little bit about
the structure of a psilocybin journey itself and relate

happening in the brain during the psilocybin journey. And then a little bit later, we
will return to that serotonin to a receptor. When we talk about some of the
more lasting changes in brain chemistry and brain wiring,
that occur after the psilocybin journey is over. So let's take a
couple of minutes and just discuss the
various components of an effective therapeutic
psilocybin journey. And here I'm not

for the treatment of depression and for other compulsive
and addictive disorders. And those data, meaning
the specific data related to those trials and
the references themselves, we'll get into a
